questions.question: The `id` command can print information about a named user or about the running process when no user is specified.
questions.explanation: The manual says `id` prints information about the given user, or about the running process if no user is specified.

questions.question: For `id`, a plain numeric-looking argument is always treated as a user ID even when a matching user name exists.
questions.explanation: Name lookup takes precedence in `id` unless the ID is explicitly marked with a leading `+`.

questions.question: By default, `id` omits the real user ID and shows only the effective user ID.
questions.explanation: The default `id` output includes the real user ID.

questions.question: By default, `id` never prints any group-related information.
questions.explanation: The default `id` output includes the real group ID and can include other group information.

questions.question: By default, `id` prints the effective user ID only when it differs from the real user ID.
questions.explanation: The manual says `id` includes the effective user ID in default output if it differs from the real user ID.

questions.question: The `id -g` option prints only the user ID.
questions.explanation: `id -g` prints only the group ID, not the user ID.

questions.question: The `id -G` option prints only the user ID and omits all group information.
questions.explanation: `id -G` prints the group ID and the supplementary groups.

questions.question: The `id -n` option prints names instead of numeric IDs, but it must be used with `-u`, `-g`, or `-G`.
questions.explanation: The manual says `id -n` requires `-u`, `-g`, or `-G`.

questions.question: The `id -r` option switches output to real IDs instead of effective IDs, and it requires `-u`, `-g`, or `-G`.
questions.explanation: `id -r` selects real IDs and must be combined with another selector option.

questions.question: The `id -u` option prints only the current group ID.
questions.explanation: `id -u` prints only the user ID.

questions.question: The `id -Z` option prints the process security context together with the normal default `id` fields.
questions.explanation: The manual says `id -Z` prints only the process security context.

questions.question: If neither SELinux nor SMACK is enabled, `id -Z` still succeeds quietly with exit status 0.
questions.explanation: Without SELinux or SMACK, `id -Z` prints a warning and sets the exit status to 1.

questions.question: The `id --zero` option uses ASCII NUL characters as output delimiters.
questions.explanation: `id --zero` changes delimiters to ASCII NUL characters.

questions.question: The `id --zero` option is allowed when using the default `id` output format.
questions.explanation: The manual says `id --zero` is not permitted with the default format.

questions.question: Within an existing login session, `id` may fail to reflect changes made later to the group database.
questions.explanation: The manual warns that `id` normally does not reflect those changes within the same login session.

questions.question: Running `id` with a user argument causes the user and group database to be consulted afresh.
questions.explanation: Supplying a user argument to `id` triggers a fresh database lookup.

questions.question: The `groups` command prints primary and supplementary group names for a given user, or for the current process if no names are given.
questions.explanation: That is the purpose of `groups` according to the manual.
questions.source_section: 20.4 groups: Print group names a user is in

questions.question: When `groups` is given more than one user name, it prints only the group lists and never prefixes them with user names.
questions.explanation: With multiple users, `groups` prints each user name before that user's group list, separated by a colon.
questions.source_section: 20.4 groups: Print group names a user is in

questions.question: The group list printed by `groups` is equivalent to the output of `id -Gn`.
questions.explanation: The manual explicitly states that `groups` output is equivalent to `id -Gn`.
questions.source_section: 20.4 groups: Print group names a user is in

questions.question: After login, `groups` always reflects any group-database changes immediately within the existing login session.
questions.explanation: The manual says `groups` normally does not reflect those changes in the existing session.
questions.source_section: 20.4 groups: Print group names a user is in

questions.question: Running `groups` with explicit user names causes the user and group database to be consulted afresh.
questions.explanation: Supplying user names to `groups` triggers a fresh database lookup.
questions.source_section: 20.4 groups: Print group names a user is in

questions.question: The `users` command prints a blank-separated single-line list of users currently logged in to the current host.
questions.explanation: That is the standard output format described for `users`.
questions.source_section: 20.5 users: Print login names of users currently logged in

questions.question: In `users` output, a user with two login sessions is listed only once.
questions.explanation: Each occurrence in `users` output corresponds to a login session, so multiple sessions repeat the name.
questions.source_section: 20.5 users: Print login names of users currently logged in

questions.question: With no file argument, `users` reads its information from a system-maintained file.
questions.explanation: The manual says `users` uses a system-maintained file when no file argument is given.
questions.source_section: 20.5 users: Print login names of users currently logged in

questions.question: If a file argument is given to `users`, it must still ignore that file and read only the default login record file.
questions.explanation: When a file argument is given, `users` uses that file instead; `/var/log/wtmp` is a common choice.
questions.source_section: 20.5 users: Print login names of users currently logged in

questions.question: With no non-option arguments, `who` prints login name, terminal line, login time, and remote hostname or X display for each logged-on user.
questions.explanation: That is the default `who` output described in the manual.
questions.source_section: 20.6 who: Print who is currently logged in

questions.question: If `who` is given one non-option argument, it uses that file instead of the default system-maintained file.
questions.explanation: A single non-option argument tells `who` to read from that file.
questions.source_section: 20.6 who: Print who is currently logged in

questions.question: With two non-option arguments such as `am i`, `who` prints every logged-in user but omits the hostname.
questions.explanation: With two non-option arguments, `who` prints only the current user's entry and precedes it with the hostname.
questions.source_section: 20.6 who: Print who is currently logged in

questions.question: The `who --boot` option prints the current number of logged-in users.
questions.explanation: `who --boot` prints the date and time of the last system boot.
questions.source_section: 20.6 who: Print who is currently logged in

questions.question: The `who --count` option prints only the login names and the number of users logged on, and it overrides all other options.
questions.explanation: The manual says `who --count` overrides all other options.
questions.source_section: 20.6 who: Print who is currently logged in
